text: In the laparoscopic splenectomy, the surgeon brings the spleen forward to expose the hilum, preparing to safely divide and ligate the vessels.
The theatre layout is shown here, both for the equipment and for the theatre staff.
The video shows the cannula positions for the laparoscopic splenectomy, with an 11mm cannula placed at the umbilical port for the introduction of the laparoscope.
The surgeon replaces the initial eleven millimeter umbilical port cannula with a twenty-five millimeter cannula to facilitate the extraction of the spleen during the laparoscopic splenectomy.

The patient is positioned on the operating table at a 45-degree angle with the left side elevated, allowing for side-to-side angulation to adjust the patient's position from horizontal to right lateral as needed.

The spleen is elevated using an atraumatic retractor while the operating table is rolled, causing the stomach to move away from the hilum due to gravity, thereby enhancing the visualization of the splenic pedicle.
The patients demonstrated in this video all have benign hematological disorders with relatively normal sized spleens. A number of approaches to laparoscopic splenectomy have been described.
The surgeon divides the splenic artery before approaching the spleen. The lesser sac is opened by incising the gastro-splenic ligament, and the short gastric vessels are clipped and divided.
The surgeon isolates the splenic pedicle and divides the splenic artery using absolute locking PDS clips, ensuring secure closure of the vessel.
The surgeon dissects along the lower pole of the spleen, clipping and dividing individual vessels while advancing towards the hilum.
The surgeon begins dissection at the lower pole of the spleen, lifting the lower edge with a flexible curved retractor. The splenic artery remains intact at this stage.
The surgeon repeats the clipping process at the splenic end of the vessel before dividing it, ensuring secure ligation prior to separation.

The surgeon uses curved forceps with a flexible shaft, similar to the retractors, to manipulate tissues within the abdomen, adjusting the curve of the forceps to a predetermined shape for optimal maneuverability.

The surgeon uses diathermy to dissect a larger vessel, passing petal and forceps around the back of the vessel to ensure it is clear. A catgut ligature is placed around one end of the vessel and tied externally.
The surgeon narrows the diameter of the vessel, allowing it to be doubly clipped with large metal clips before being divided.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the upper limb of the spleen, elevating the lower pole with a flexible curved retractor. Vessels and peritoneal attachments are stretched and safely dissected or divided, allowing adjacent structures to visibly separate from the spleen.

The surgeon opens the gastrosplenic ligament to gain control of the hilum, passing curved forceps under the remaining pedicle. The lower pole of the spleen is already devascularized. A linear thread is passed percutaneously to lift the pedicle, while a curved retractor draws the tail of the pancreas medially before the pedicle is divided.
The surgeon uses a sizer to assess the thickness of the splenic pedicle before employing an endoscopic GIA stapler to divide it.
The surgeon continues dissection close to the spleen, individually clipping and dividing branches of the splenic vessels, following the early division of the splenic artery.
The surgeon uses a retractor to lift the spleen away from its attachments, facilitating further dissection and separation from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon displays the lino-renal ligament by suspending the spleen and applying counter traction with a second retractor, sweeping the intraperitoneal structures medially. Diathermy dissection is performed with tissues held under tension.

Under magnification, the surgeon clips small branches of the splenic artery individually while dissecting around the hilum. The tail of the pancreas is visible and remains undamaged during this close dissection near the spleen.
The surgeon lifts the spleen to divide the posterior leaf of the linoleum ligament, detaching the final remaining attachment.

The surgeon places the spleen into a tough plastic intravenous infusion bag, which is inserted into the abdomen through a 25mm cannula. The edges of the bag are brought up to the surface through the cannula.

The surgeon digitally breaks up the spleen within the intravenous infusion bag and removes it in several pieces using sponge holding forceps. The suspended pedicle technique is employed during the laparoscopic splenectomy.
